Motion For New Trial And For Reconsideration
Off Calendar Oct-10-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: Plaintiff's motion for new trial and motion for reconsideration.

Appearances (via Zoom):

Stan Schuldner, Plaintiff in propria persona.

Zachary Frampton, Esq., HOLLAND & KNIGHT LLP, (213) 896-2400, for Defendants.

Ruling is argued. At conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling. "Plaintiff's motion for new trial and motion for reconsideration" is denied. A reconsideration motion must be "based upon new or different facts, circumstances, or law" and a valid reason for not presenting them earlier. (CCP Sec. 1008; Gilberd v. AC Transit (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1500; New York Times Co. v. Sup. Ct. (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 206, 212-13.) No new or different facts, circumstances or law that could not have been presented earlier are presented now, just already adjudicated arguments. Indeed, the word "reconsideration" appears nowhere in plaintiff's memorandum beyond its first sentence. Likewise, the first page of plaintiff's memorandum lists "grounds" for a new trial, but never again mentions them. No effort is made to tie the "grounds" to any facts, circumstances or law, and the court is not required to speculate.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Proceeding Reported by "For the Record". (302/RBU)

***After hearing, an order is signed.

MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
Off Calendar Jul-26-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Plaintiff STAN SCHULDNER in pro per;
Attys. ABRAHAM J. COLMAN, Esq. and ZACHARY C. FRAMPTON, Esq. of Holland & Knight LLP (Tel: 213-896-2400) for defendants Incomm Financial Services, Inc. and the Bancorp Bank.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS THE BANCORP BANK, AND INCOMM FINANCIAL SERVICES' MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' (THE BANCORP BANK, INCOMM FINANCIAL SERVICES) motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied.

Defendant argues that claim preclusion bars this action. (See DKN Holdings LLC v. Faerber (2015) 61 Cal.4th 813, 824 [California Supreme Court indicated a preference for the term "claim preclusion" rather than "res judicata"].) "Claim preclusion arises if a second suit involves: (1) the same cause of action (2) between the same parties (3) after a final judgment on the merits in the first suit." (DKN Holdings LLC, 61 Cal.4th at 824.)

In this case, plaintiff alleges that the parties' 2018 credit card agreement retroactively superseded the 2013 credit card agreement and defendants engaged in other wrongdoing during the course of the litigation in CPF-15-514368. (Amended Complaint, pars. 5, 7, 14.) Plaintiff's fifth amended cross-complaint in CPF-15-514368 did not mention the 2018 credit card agreement and the import of that agreement could not be litigated in that action since it arose after that case had been litigated for years. (See Flood v. Simpson (1975) 45 Cal.App.3d 644, 647 [explaining that a supplemental complaint, which pleads facts that arise after an action is commenced, cannot plead new causes of action].) This case therefore involves different "causes of action" and claim preclusion does not apply. (See Allied Fire Protection v. Diede Construction, Inc. (2005) 127 Cal.App.4th 150, 155 [Claim preclusion "is not a bar to claims that arise after the initial complaint is filed."]; Neil Norman, Ltd. v. William Kasper & Co. (1983) 149 Cal.App.3d 942, 947 [suit complaining about defects with wool sweaters did not bar subsequent suit regarding defects in acrylic sweaters that were delivered after the first suit was filed].) "Under well settled law, the doctrine of res judicata does not apply where there are changed conditions and new facts which were not in existence at the time of the prior judgment, and upon which such judgment was based." (Starr v. City and County of San Francisco (1977) 72 Cal.App.3d 164, 178-179.)

On January 15, 2020, this court stayed this case and subsequently entered an interlocutory judgment based on CCP 430.10(c) and the doctrine of exclusive concurrent jurisdiction. The court granted that motion as unopposed. While the reference to CCP 430.10(c) suggests that the same primary rights were at stake in both cases, as explained above, they were not. Moreover, the doctrine of exclusive concurrent jurisdiction is far broader than a plea of abatement per CCP 430.10(c) and the stay was properly issued under that doctrine. (See Plant Insulation Co. v. Fibreboard Corp. (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 781, 788.)

The court presently has no opinion regarding the import of the 2018 credit card agreement or whether plaintiff is properly characterizing it since the moving party failed to raise that issue in its moving papers.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Motion For New Trial
Off Calendar Jun-18-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Plaintiff STAN SCHULDNER (Tel: 415-724-6862) appearing via CourtCall in pro per;
Atty. MATHEW M. WRENSHALL, Esq. of Reed Smith LLP (Tel: 213-457-8076) appearing via CourtCall for defendants Incomm Financial Services, Inc., Bankcorp Bank, and Reed Smith LLP.

Hearing Re: Plaintiff's Motion For New Trial.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Plaintiff Schuldiner's motion for new trial is denied. Plaintiff fails to demonstrate that the court's prior ruling on defendant's anti-SLAPP motion to strike and the subsequent judgment were legally erroneous.

The court did not err in denying plaintiff leave to amend to plead a completely new cause of action in response to defendant's anti-SLAPP motion to strike. "[A] plaintiff cannot use an eleventh hour amendment to plead around a motion to strike under the anti-SLAPP statute." (Navellier v. Sletten (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 763, 772-773 [allowing amendment in response to anti-SLAPP motion would undermine anti-SLAPP statute]; accord, Salma v. Capon (2008) 161 Cal.App.4th 1275, 1280, 1294 [holding that a plaintiff may not avoid an anti-SLAPP motion to strike by amending the challenged complaint before the motion to strike is heard].) Plaintiff's reliance on Nguyen-Lam v. Sinh Cuong Cao (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 858 is misplaced. In Nguyen-Lam, the trial court allowed plaintiff to amend her complaint to plead malice to support her defamation cause of action. The Court of Appeal found no error because the amendment did not frustrate the purpose of the anti-SLAPP statute. The Court of Appeal explained that in response to the defendant's anti-SLAPP motion, the plaintiff presented evidence to support each element of her defamation claim-including malice-and therefore demonstrated a probability of prevailing on her claim. In those circumstances, the amendment did not undermine the anti-SLAPP statute because the plaintiff presented evidence of a viable claim and the court allowed amendment merely to conform to proof. (See Hecimovich v. Encinal School Parent Teacher Organization (2012) 203 Cal.App.4th 450, 461-462 [Nguyen-Lam involved unusual situation where "the evidence showing that plaintiff could prevail on the merits was before the trial court at the time of the motion].) In this case, allowing amendment will lead to another round of pleadings, further delay, increased costs, and another anti-SLAPP motion to strike. Amendment is therefore inappropriate. (See Nguyen-Lam, 171 Cal.App.4th at 872.)

As explained in the order granting defendant's anti-SLAPP motion to strike, the litigation privilege bars plaintiff's extortion claim. The alleged extortion is based on communications made after litigation was commenced and during the course of a deposition. Such litigation communications are absolutely privileged. "This privilege is absolute in nature, applying 'to all publications, irrespective of their maliciousness.' [Citation.]" (Action Apartment Assn., Inc. v. City of Santa Monica (2007) 41 Cal.4th 1232, 1241.) " 'Any doubt about whether the privilege applies is resolved in favor of applying it.' [Citation.]" (Finton Construction, Inv. v. Bidna & Keys, APLC (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 200, 212; see also Flatley v. Mauro (2006) 39 Cal.4th 299, 322 ["The litigation privilege has been applied in 'numerous cases' involving 'fraudulent communication or perjured testimony.'"].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
